questions.text: I find that the gap between something happening and my emotional response is nearly zero.
I'd rather start doing the work immediately than spend time building systems first.
I commit to a decision and adjust quickly if the outcome isn't what I expected.
I often realize only later that someone was upset during a conversation.
I am uncomfortable using veto power, even when I think I see a better path.
I experience the world as emotionally legible but not emotionally demanding.
I hold the view that giving genuine moral weight to others' pain — treating it as a priority — is a practice that requires deliberate cultivation.
I always carry items I might need — a charger, a pen, an umbrella — just in case.
I find that the obligation to respond to suffering is one of the few moral intuitions I trust completely.
I think that approaching painful situations with methodical objectivity is the approach most consistent with treating all parties with fairness and respect.
I rarely notice when someone is building consensus behind the scenes before a formal discussion.
I am comfortable making big bets on behalf of a group.
I work to ensure that access to key decisions is open to everyone involved.
I find that my emotional responses sometimes feel disproportionate, yet entirely genuine.
I find that volunteering my time for others gives me more fulfillment than personal leisure.
I feel most like myself when I am in the middle of helping someone.
I notice that I can feel the physiological echo of a strong emotion for days after the event that caused it.
I tend to frame career conversations around my own aspirations and what I need to get there.
I feel that my emotional system runs with a kind of quiet efficiency.
I detect subtle territorial behaviors like someone adjusting objects on a shared table.
I detect when a group member's contribution is being quietly dismissed by the way others respond to it.
I find that I can provide more sustained and equitable support by not becoming emotionally invested in any single case.
I believe my steady nervous system is one of the most consistent things about me across all contexts.
I hold the view that emotional distance during times of crisis is not a form of avoidance but a deliberate stance that allows for more careful and more effective engagement.
I believe that personal convenience should never come at the expense of someone else's basic need.
I set recurring calendar events for tasks that need to happen regularly.
I believe that the moral weight of suffering should be the primary lens through which governments and organizations evaluate their policies.
I believe that my personal standards for quality should never be compromised to accommodate others.
I enjoy being the person who approves or vetoes ideas.
I believe that too much planning can be just as harmful as too little.
I believe that preparation is a form of respect — for the task, for the people involved, and for myself.
I can tell when someone is smiling politely but is actually upset.
I believe that if I don't invest in myself, no one else will.
I find it satisfying to work as part of a peer network without reporting lines.
I tend not to register the social dynamics of who gets the last word in a conversation.
I want to be the one who shapes how a project unfolds.
